author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
A Treatise on the Gift of Perseverance.
Faith is the Beginning of a Christian Man. Martyrdom for Christ’s Sake is His Best Ending.
originalBook: A Treatise on the Gift of Perseverance.
title: Faith is the Beginning of a Christian Man. Martyrdom for Christ’s Sake is His Best Ending.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Chapter 2 [II.]—Faith is the Beginning of a Christian Man.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Martyrdom for Christ’s Sake is His Best Ending.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: This matter being settled, let us see whether this perseverance, of which it was said, “He that persevereth unto the end, the same shall be saved,” is a gift of God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And if it be not, how is that saying of the apostle true: “Unto you it is given in the behalf of Christ, not only to believe on Him, but also to suffer for His sake”?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Of these things, certainly, one has respect to the beginning, the other to the end.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Yet each is the gift of God, because both are said to be given; as, also, I have already said above.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For what is more truly the beginning for a Christian than to believe in Christ?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: What end is better than to suffer for Christ?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But so far as pertains to believing in Christ, whatever kind of contradiction has been discovered, that not the beginning but the increase of faith should be called God’s gift,—to this opinion, by God’s gift, I have answered enough, and more than enough.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But what reason can be given why perseverance to the end should not be given in Christ to him to whom it is given to suffer for Christ, or, to speak more distinctly, to whom it is given to die for Christ?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For the Apostle Peter, showing that this is the gift of God, says, “It is better, if the will of God be so, to suffer for well-doing than for evil-doing.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: When he says, “If the will of God be so,” he shows that this is divinely given, and yet not to all saints, to suffer for Christ’s sake.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For certainly those whom the will of God does not will to attain to the experience and the glory of suffering, do not fail to attain to the kingdom of God if they persevere in Christ to the end.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But who can say that this perseverance is not given to those who die in Christ from any weakness of body, or by any kind of accident, although a far more difficult perseverance is given to those by whom even death itself is undergone for Christ’s sake?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Because perseverance is much more difficult when the persecutor is engaged in preventing a man’s perseverance; and therefore he is sustained in his perseverance unto death.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Hence it is more difficult to have the former perseverance,—easier to have the latter; but to Him to whom nothing is difficult it is easy to give both.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For God has promised this, saying, “I will put my fear in their hearts, that they may not depart from me.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And what else is this than, “Such and so great shall be my fear that I will put into their hearts that they will perseveringly cleave to me”?
